title: Fox Sports Deploys Five Alexi Lalas Lookalikes Through Boston Marathon in World Cup Countdown Stunt
detail: Fox Sports staged a guerrilla marketing campaign at the 2026 Boston Marathon on Patriots' Day (April 20), sending five runners costumed as 1994 USMNT legend Alexi Lalas — complete with his signature denim-style kit, flowing red hair, and long goatee — through the 26.2-mile race. The stunt served as promotion for Fox's exclusive World Cup broadcast rights, with Gillette Stadium in Foxborough, Massachusetts — located near the marathon's finish area — hosting seven World Cup matches. With 52 days to kickoff, Fox Sports has intensified its World Cup marketing campaign, connecting the tournament's New England hub to one of the most-watched events on the American sports calendar. The real Alexi Lalas serves as a FIFA World Cup 2026 studio analyst for Fox Sports.

media.caption: Five Alexi Lalas lookalikes run the 2026 Boston Marathon as part of Fox Sports' FIFA World Cup 2026 broadcast promotion

title: Kansas City's $15 World Cup Round-Trip Shuttle Offers Sharp Contrast to New Jersey's $150 Train Fare
detail: While NJ Transit's $150 round-trip train fare from Penn Station to MetLife Stadium continued to dominate national headlines following its April 17 announcement, Kansas City published its own World Cup match-day transit plan on April 20 — offering fans a $15 round-trip shuttle service from designated park-and-ride lots and a $5 unlimited-day bus pass. The contrast became an instant point of comparison: Kansas City fans attending matches at Arrowhead Stadium will pay approximately 10 times less than New York-area fans traveling to MetLife. The stark difference spotlights the unequal economic burden placed on fans depending on which of the 16 host venues they attend. Kansas City hosts eight World Cup matches including a semifinal and Round of 32 games. Other host cities including Atlanta (MARTA flat fare), Dallas, Houston, and Philadelphia have similarly maintained standard or modestly elevated transit fares. The NJ Transit backlash — including criticism from FIFA's COO, NJ Governor Sherrill, and Senator Chuck Schumer — continued into its fourth day with no reversal.

media.caption: Kansas City's $15 round-trip World Cup shuttle plan contrasts with New Jersey's $150 NJ Transit fare
